impacts.caseName: Teva Pharmaceuticals International GmbH v. Eli Lilly and Company
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court correctly granted JMOL that Teva's headache patents claiming methods of using humanized anti-CGRP antagonist antibodies to treat headache were invalid for failure to satisfy the written-description and enablement requirements of 35 U.S.C. § 112.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's JMOL of invalidity, reinstating a jury verdict that Lilly willfully infringed Teva's patents covering anti-CGRP antibody treatments for headache. The case involves blockbuster migraine drugs (Teva's Ajovy and Lilly's competing product) and has major implications for the scope of patent protection for biological therapies.
impacts.positiveImplications: Strengthens patent protection for biopharmaceutical innovators by establishing that patents claiming a genus of humanized antibodies with known methods of making them can satisfy written description and enablement, even when only one specific antibody is disclosed in the specification.
impacts.negativeImplications: Competitors developing biosimilar or follow-on biologic products face greater risk of infringement liability where genus claims to antibody therapies are upheld despite limited species disclosure.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder that conditions patent licenses on customers' exclusive purchase of the patent holder's unpatented products engages in unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, even when the patent is later invalidated.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act. Ingevity had conditioned licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers exclusively purchasing Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the antitrust liability and corresponding damages award.
impacts.positiveImplications: Protects competitors like BASF from being locked out of markets through patent-leveraged tying arrangements, reinforcing that patent rights cannot be used to coerce purchases of unpatented products.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who bundle licensing with product purchase requirements face significant antitrust liability, even where the underlying patent covers related technology. Companies must carefully structure patent licensing to avoid tying claims.

impacts.caseName: Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to detecting anomalous program executions by comparing function calls in an emulator to a model of expected behavior are directed to an abstract idea under Alice step one, and whether the district court erred in denying JMOL on damages for foreign sales of cybersecurity software.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated the $185 million jury verdict for Columbia, holding that the asserted cybersecurity patent claims are abstract at Alice step one and remanding for step two analysis. The court also held that damages based on Norton's foreign software sales were improperly awarded, limiting extraterritorial patent damages.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies accused of infringing software patents gain a stronger basis to challenge eligibility of claims directed to detecting anomalous behavior through comparison models. The foreign sales ruling limits damages exposure for global software distribution.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the cybersecurity and software detection space face heightened § 101 eligibility challenges. Universities and other patent owners may recover significantly less in damages where accused products are sold globally.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to a server that tailors content delivery specifications to a wireless device's screen size or capabilities are ineligible for patenting under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's patents claiming server-based content tailoring for wireless devices are ineligible under § 101, reversing the jury's infringement finding and $2.5 million damages award. The court found the claims directed to abstract ideas, requiring entry of judgment for Netflix.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming and content delivery companies gain protection from patent assertions based on broadly claimed content adaptation methods, which the court found abstract.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims directed to adaptive content delivery and device-specific content rendering face substantial § 101 invalidity risk, undermining monetization of such portfolios.

impacts.caseName: International Medical Devices, Inc. v. Cornell
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the jury had a legally sufficient evidentiary basis to find that four asserted trade secrets were protectable under California law, and whether ideas that are generally known in the field can constitute an inventive contribution for patent inventorship purposes.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the trade secret misappropriation verdict, finding insufficient evidence that the asserted trade secrets were protectable because the underlying ideas were generally known. The court also reversed the patent invalidity ruling on inventorship, as the alleged inventor's contribution of generally known ideas could not constitute inventive contribution. The counterfeiting verdict was affirmed.
impacts.positiveImplications: Defendants in trade secret cases benefit from heightened scrutiny of whether claimed secrets are truly protectable and not merely generally known information. The ruling also clarifies that generally known ideas cannot support inventorship claims to invalidate competitor patents.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies relying on trade secret protection for knowledge that may be generally known in their field face greater risk of losing protection. The decision also limits the ability to challenge patent inventorship based on contributions of well-known concepts.

impacts.caseName: VLSI Technology LLC v. Intel Corporation
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court correctly granted summary judgment of noninfringement based on extraterritoriality where the accused multi-core processor selection method was implemented in chips designed in the U.S. but manufactured and largely sold abroad, and whether doctrine of equivalents infringement was properly rejected.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed-in-part the summary judgment for Intel on extraterritoriality grounds and remanded, finding genuine disputes of material fact regarding whether sufficient steps of the patented method occurred in the United States. The court affirmed the rejection of VLSI's doctrine of equivalents theory.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders suing semiconductor and technology companies with global operations may pursue infringement claims where meaningful steps of a patented method occur domestically, even if manufacturing and sales are primarily overseas.
impacts.negativeImplications: Global technology companies face continued uncertainty regarding the territorial reach of U.S. method patents, particularly where design and testing occur domestically but manufacturing is offshore.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the on-sale bar of pre-AIA 35 U.S.C. § 102(b) requires that a commercial sale expressly disclose the prior art device's functionality, and whether a Rule 30(b)(6) corporate representative may testify about historical sales beyond personal knowledge at summary judgment.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed summary judgment of patent invalidity under the on-sale bar, holding that the bar does not require sales to expressly disclose functionality of the prior art device, and that 30(b)(6) deposition testimony is admissible at summary judgment even if the witness lacks personal knowledge. The court also held that computer source code is not hearsay.
impacts.positiveImplications: Defendants challenging patents under the on-sale bar benefit from the ruling that prior commercial sales need not expressly disclose device functionality. The 30(b)(6) ruling also makes it easier to prove historical sales through corporate representatives.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face broader invalidity risk under the on-sale bar, as any commercial sale of an embodying device before the critical date may invalidate a patent regardless of whether the buyer knew the device's specific patented functionality.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether summary judgment was properly granted on inequitable conduct and Walker Process fraud claims related to the patentee's failure to disclose prior art documents (the CYMAX Documents) during patent prosecution.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both inequitable conduct and Walker Process fraud, finding genuine disputes of material fact precluded resolution at summary judgment. The case involves Tenaris's coiled tubing patents and prior art documents from an acquired company that were not disclosed to the USPTO.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors challenging patents obtained through potentially inequitable prosecution gain broader access to trial on both inequitable conduct and antitrust-based Walker Process fraud claims when material prior art was acquired but not disclosed.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that acquire patent portfolios along with business assets face increased litigation risk if prior art documents from the acquired company were not disclosed during prosecution of related patents.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC correctly found that Apple's importation and sale of Apple Watch models with blood oxygen measurement features violated § 337 of the Tariff Act by infringing Masimo's patents covering wearable blood oxygen measurement devices.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's finding that Apple violated § 337 by importing Apple Watches that infringed Masimo's patents on wearable blood oxygen measurement technology, upholding the limited exclusion order. The court found no error in the domestic industry determination, validity rulings, or infringement findings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders with domestic industry investments in wearable health technology gain confidence that the ITC remains an effective forum for excluding infringing imports of consumer electronics devices.
impacts.negativeImplications: Major consumer electronics companies integrating health-monitoring features face significant supply chain risk from ITC exclusion orders and must carefully evaluate freedom-to-operate before incorporating patented sensor technologies.

impacts.caseName: Ascendis Pharma A/S v. BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an ITC respondent may obtain a mandatory stay under 28 U.S.C. § 1659(a)(2) of a refiled declaratory judgment action involving the same parties when it previously missed the statutory deadline to seek a stay of the original action.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that BioMarin could not obtain a mandatory stay of Ascendis's refiled declaratory judgment action under § 1659(a)(2) when it had missed the deadline to seek a stay of the original action. The ruling prevents ITC respondents from circumventing statutory deadlines through procedural maneuvering.
impacts.positiveImplications: Pharmaceutical and biotech companies seeking declaratory judgment of non-infringement can proceed in district court without mandatory stays when ITC respondents miss statutory deadlines, preserving access to the safe harbor defense under 35 U.S.C. § 271(e)(1).
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who file ITC complaints and miss the § 1659(a)(2) deadline cannot cure the deficiency by dismissing and refiling district court actions to reset the clock, limiting strategic options for managing parallel proceedings.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent plaintiff's damages expert report and granting summary judgment based on absence of a remedy.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report and vacated summary judgment for Microsoft, holding that the district court applied too stringent a standard in excluding the expert's reasonable royalty analysis. The decision reinforces that damages experts should not be excluded merely for relying on benefits-based approaches to quantify patent damages.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent plaintiffs gain greater flexibility in presenting damages theories, as courts should not exclude expert testimony solely because it quantifies benefits of the patented technology rather than relying on comparable licenses.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies defending patent infringement suits face increased difficulty in excluding damages expert testimony at the Daubert stage, potentially leading to larger damages exposure at trial.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTAB correctly construed claims of a patent directed to streaming partly encrypted media content, specifically the scope of 'encryption information' in the context of adaptive bitrate streaming systems.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the PTAB's claim construction of DivX's streaming encryption patent, vacated the Board's decision rejecting Netflix's obviousness challenge, and remanded for further proceedings under the corrected construction. The ruling has implications for how encryption-related patent claims are interpreted in media streaming technology.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming technology companies challenging encryption-related patents benefit from narrower claim constructions that may render such patents obvious over prior art, reducing royalty exposure.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in digital rights management and encrypted streaming technology may face narrower claim scope, reducing the ability to assert patents against adaptive bitrate streaming implementations.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the PTAB governing discretionary denials of inter partes review petitions constitute 'general statements of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b).
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions governing discretionary IPR institution decisions are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking. The court emphasized that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution and that the Director retains ultimate authority over institution decisions.
impacts.positiveImplications: The PTO retains broad flexibility to issue and modify guidance on IPR institution discretion without formal rulemaking, allowing responsive policy adjustments.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies relying on IPR as a cost-effective mechanism to challenge patents face continued uncertainty, as the Director's discretionary denial policies cannot be challenged as procedurally deficient under the APA.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly approved a $125 million class action settlement of claims that the federal judiciary overcharged PACER users by using fees to fund expenses unrelated to PACER operations, and whether incentive awards to class representatives were permissible.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed approval of the $125 million PACER fee class action settlement, rejecting an objector's challenges to jurisdiction, settlement fairness, attorneys' fees, and incentive awards. The court found that hundreds of thousands of PACER users would be fully or substantially reimbursed for fees incurred during the eight-year class period.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses and law firms that are heavy PACER users will receive reimbursement for excessive fees. The decision also validates incentive awards to organizational class representatives in government fee overcharge cases.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations as confidential is consistent with 19 U.S.C. § 1516a, and whether the statute abrogates the common law right of access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that the ITC's blanket practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential is incompatible with the governing statute and that § 1516a does not abrogate the common law right of access to judicial proceedings. The ruling requires the ITC to evaluate confidentiality on a case-by-case basis.
impacts.positiveImplications: Domestic producers and other parties in trade remedy proceedings gain greater access to information submitted in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations, promoting transparency in trade proceedings that significantly affect business competition.
impacts.negativeImplications: Foreign producers and importers who submit commercially sensitive data in ITC questionnaires face reduced confidentiality protections and potential disclosure of proprietary business information to competitors.
